content.heading: Scope — what can each part of your code see?

blocks.text: Scope determines which variables a piece of code can access. Variables declared outside any function are global — visible everywhere. Variables declared inside a function are local — only visible inside that function.

blocks.alt: Diagram showing global scope and local scope inside a function.

blocks.desc: Global scope — any function can read or change this
Local scope — only visible inside the function it was declared in

lines: let message = 'Round starting!';  // local — only visible here
level = level + 1;                // works — level is global
console.log(message);  // ReferenceError — message does not exist here

blocks.label: Reference Error
blocks.text: When JavaScript can't find a variable, it throws a ReferenceError. This almost always means the variable was declared inside a function and you're trying to use it outside. Check where it was declared with let.

content.heading: Watch out — same name, different variable

blocks.text: If you declare a variable inside a function with the same name as a global one, JavaScript creates a brand new local variable. The global one is untouched. This is called shadowing and it is one of the most common bugs beginners write.

lines: let level = 99;  // local — shadows the global
console.log(level);  // still 1 — the global was never touched

blocks.label: Easy bug to miss
blocks.text: If you write let level inside a function, you are creating a new local variable — not updating the global one. To change the global, just write level = 99 with no let. The let keyword is what creates a new variable.

blocks.label: Simon Says connection
blocks.text: In Simon Says, level and playerClicks are global so every function — startRound, handleClick, checkResult — can read and update them. If any of those functions declared their own local level with let, the game would break in a very confusing way.

exercises.title: What does global scope mean?
tasks: level is declared outside any function in Simon Says. What does that mean?
exercises.hint: Think about which parts of the code can read a variable declared at the very top.

options.label: Only the function directly below it can read it
Any function in the program can read and change it
It is deleted after the first function runs
It can be read but never changed

exercises.explanation: A variable declared outside any function is global — every function in the program can read and update it. That is why level and playerClicks are declared at the top in Simon Says: startRound, handleClick, and checkResult all need them.

exercises.title: Which line throws a ReferenceError?
tasks: One line in this code throws a ReferenceError. Which one?
exercises.hint: A ReferenceError means JavaScript can't find the variable. Where was it declared?

options.label: Line 3 — console.log can't be used inside a function
Line 6 — message only exists inside startRound
Line 5 — you can't call a function that way

exercises.explanation: message was declared with let inside startRound, so it only lives inside those curly braces. Line 6 is outside the function — JavaScript can't find message and throws a ReferenceError. To fix it, either log message inside startRound or declare it globally.

exercises.title: Global or local?
tasks: Sort each variable declaration into the correct bucket based on where it is declared.
exercises.hint: A variable is global if it has no surrounding curly braces. Local if it appears inside a function body.
exercises.explanation: The last item is a trap: let level = 99 inside startRound looks like it updates the global, but it creates a brand new local variable named level. The global is untouched. Any let inside a function always creates a local — that is what let does.

exercises.title: Read a global inside a function
tasks: level is declared globally. Complete showLevel so it logs the current level.
exercises.hint: To read a global variable inside a function, just use its name — no special keyword needed.
exercises.explanation: Inside a function, you read a global variable just by writing its name — level, not 'level' (that is a string), not let level (that creates a new local), and not global.level (that is not JavaScript).

exercises.title: Update the global — not a shadow
tasks: Complete nextLevel so it adds 1 to the global level without creating a shadow.
exercises.hint: The let keyword creates a new variable. Without let, you update the one that already exists.
exercises.explanation: level = level + 1 (no let) reads the global and stores the incremented value back. let level = level + 1 creates a brand new local variable named level — the global is never touched. This is the most common scope bug: forgetting to remove let.

exercises.title: Put the Simon Says setup in order
tasks: Drag these lines into the correct order so the program runs without errors and logs the right level.
exercises.hint: Global variables must be declared before the functions that use them. Functions must be defined before they are called.
exercises.explanation: Globals are declared first so the function body can reference them. The function call goes last — startRound needs to exist before you can call it. Inside startRound, there is no let before level or playerClicks, so both assignments update the globals.

exercises.title: Trace the shadow
tasks: Follow the code carefully. What do the two console.log calls print?
exercises.hint: Look for the let keyword inside the function. That is where the shadow begins.
exercises.explanation: let level = 5 inside startRound creates a completely separate local variable. The global level is never touched. Inside the function you see 5 (the local). Outside you see 1 (the global, unchanged). The local level shadows the global while inside the function, then disappears when the function ends.

exercises.title: Type the update — no let
tasks: handleClick should add 1 to the global playerClicks. Type the left side of the assignment — no let.
exercises.hint: To update a global, just write the variable name. Adding let would create a local shadow instead.
exercises.explanation: playerClicks = playerClicks + 1 reads the global (0), adds 1, and stores 1 back. Writing let playerClicks = playerClicks + 1 would create a local shadow — the global stays 0 and the check fails.

exercises.title: Which version updates the global?
tasks: level is a global variable. Which version of startRound correctly increases it by 1?
exercises.hint: Only one version modifies the global variable. The others either create a shadow, log, or return without saving.
exercises.explanation: Version B is the only one that updates the global: level = level + 1 with no let reads the current value and stores the result back. Version A creates a local shadow. Versions C and D compute the new value but never save it anywhere — the global stays unchanged.

exercises.title: Write nextLevel
tasks: Write a function called nextLevel that adds 1 to the global level.
Inside nextLevel, also reset playerClicks to 0.
Call nextLevel twice, then log level — it should print 3.
exercises.hint: No let before level or playerClicks inside the function — those are already declared globally at the top.

steps.text: Declare function nextLevel() { } with the curly braces. Hit Run.
Inside nextLevel, add level = level + 1; — no let. Hit Run.
Inside nextLevel, also add playerClicks = 0; Hit Run.
Call nextLevel(); twice, then write console.log(level); Hit Run.
level is 3 and playerClicks is 0. Both globals updated — no let inside the function, no shadowing.

exercises.explanation: Functions can read and write global variables by name with no let inside the function. level = level + 1 updates the global; let level = level + 1 would create a shadow and leave the global unchanged.

exercises.title: Make two functions share a global
tasks: Declare level as a global variable set to 1.
Write startRound — it adds 1 to level and logs the new level.
Write resetGame — it sets level back to 1 and logs it.
Call startRound twice, resetGame once, then startRound once more. Log level at the end — it should print 2.
exercises.hint: Both functions use level without let inside their bodies. The let goes only at the very top when the variable is first created.

steps.text: Declare let level = 1; at the top. Hit Run.
Write function startRound() that adds 1 to level (no let) and logs 'Level: ' + level. Hit Run.
Write function resetGame() that sets level = 1 and logs 'Reset to level: ' + level. Hit Run.
Call startRound(); twice, resetGame();, then startRound(); once more. Log level. Hit Run.
Two functions, one shared global. That is how Simon Says keeps level in sync across every function.

checks.name: level
checks.label: level ends at 2 after the full sequence

exercises.explanation: Both startRound and resetGame update the same global level. Neither uses let inside the function body — let was only needed once, at the top when the variable was first created.

exercises.title: Build the Simon Says level tracker
tasks: Write handleClick — it adds 1 to playerClicks and logs the total.
Write nextLevel — it adds 1 to level and resets playerClicks to 0.
Write checkWin — if playerClicks equals level, log 'Round won! Level: ' + level, then call nextLevel().
Simulate a round: call handleClick three times, then checkWin. Log level at the end.
exercises.hint: All three functions use level and playerClicks with no let inside them. The globals are declared once at the very top.

steps.text: Write function handleClick() — it adds 1 to playerClicks and logs 'Clicks: ' + playerClicks. Hit Run.
Write function nextLevel() — adds 1 to level and resets playerClicks = 0. Hit Run.
Write function checkWin() — if playerClicks === level, log 'Round won! Level: ' + level and call nextLevel(). Hit Run.
Call handleClick() three times, then checkWin(). Log level at the end. Hit Run.
Three functions, two shared globals, zero let inside the function bodies. That is scope working for you — not against you.

exercises.explanation: All three functions share level and playerClicks without declaring their own local copies. This is the scope pattern Simon Says depends on: globals declared once at the top, multiple functions reading and updating them.

challenges.title: The level never increases
challenges.description: The function should increase the global level by 1 each time it runs. But level is always 1 no matter how many times you call it. Find the single bug.
challenges.hint: Look at the let keyword inside the function. What does let do?
challenges.explanation: let level inside the function creates a brand new local variable that shadows the global one. The global level is never touched. Fix: remove the let so it reads level = level + 1. Now the function updates the global variable instead of creating a new local one.

challenges.title: The score display can't see the score
challenges.description: The function updateDisplay should show the current score on screen but it throws a ReferenceError. Find why and fix it without moving the console.log.
challenges.hint: score is local to setScore. updateDisplay can't see inside another function. Where would score need to live so both functions can see it?
challenges.explanation: score is declared inside setScore with let, making it local. updateDisplay is a completely separate function — it can't see inside setScore. Fix: move let score = 42 outside both functions so it is global. Now both functions can read it.

challenges.title: Three functions, one broken variable
challenges.description: A Simon Says game has three functions that all need to read and update level. Right now only one of them works correctly. Find all the scope bugs and fix them so all three functions share the same global level.
challenges.hint: Look for let inside functions. Each one creates a shadow. resetGame is the only one doing it correctly.
challenges.explanation: startRound and displayLevel both use let level inside the function, creating local shadows instead of touching the global. resetGame is the only correct one — no let, so it updates the global. Fix: remove let from both startRound and displayLevel so all three functions read and write the same global level.
